What each does:

dir_summary — summarize one directory under the configured root

find_files — find files by glob and optional name/path substring

latest_modified_files — list newest modified files

file_info — metadata for one file by id or relative path

grep_exact — exact string search across files

grep_regex — regex search across files

read_lines — read an exact line range from one file

read_neighbors — read a window around a specific line

py_outline — compact Python outline for one file

py_symbol_search — search Python symbols by name

py_read_symbol — read exact source for one Python symbol

py_imports — list imports used by a Python module

explain the nrc adams aps api pipelien from ingestion to farthest downstream

I’m treating this as a request about the local project connector, not public NRC documentation. I’ll trace the concrete code path for ADAMS/APS/API from ingestion through the furthest downstream consumer, then summarize where the evidence is thin if the naming is inconsistent.
I’m tracing the repo’s references to the NRC ADAMS APS API pipeline, starting with exact term searches and then following the most relevant files downstream.

I found the artifact lineage quickly: evidence bundle → citation pack → evidence report → report export → context packet/dossier. Now I’m locating the code/spec definitions that sit upstream of those generated JSONs, so the explanation is about the pipeline design rather than just one run artifact.

Interpretation: you mean the repo’s modeled pipeline around the NRC ADAMS Public Search API (APS), not a general description of the public NRC service. I do not have clean source-level proof for every hop because the connector let me search and list artifacts but then failed on direct file reads. So what follows is the strongest artifact-backed reconstruction, not a guaranteed full orchestration trace.
The shortest correct answer is this:
APS request → evidence bundle → citation pack → evidence report → report export → context packet → context dossier
That is the downstream chain I can actually defend from the repo.
End-to-end pipeline, from ingestion to furthest downstream

External ingestion source
The repo contains an API dossier for “NRC ADAMS Public Search API (APS)” and identifies the base URL as https://adams-api.nrc.gov. So the upstream data source is explicitly the NRC ADAMS APS search API, not a scrape-only pipeline.

Request ingestion / normalization
The first concrete pipeline artifact I can see is aps_evidence_bundle_v2.
Its metadata shows:

request_contract_id: aps_evidence_request_norm_v1

request_normalization_contract_id: aps_evidence_request_norm_v1

That means the first internal step is not “raw API call output” as a durable artifact. It is a normalized evidence request, then a bundle build around that request.

Evidence ingestion and structural preprocessing
Inside the evidence bundle lineage, I can see repeated references to:

aps_text_normalization_v2

aps_chunking_v2

aps_content_units_v2

aps_evidence_ranking_v1

So the ingestion stage is doing at least four things:

normalize retrieved text

chunk it

turn it into content units

rank/select evidence

This is the real “ingestion” layer in repo terms. It is already post-retrieval and post-cleaning.

Evidence bundle
The first major durable artifact is:

aps_evidence_bundle_v2

This looks like the canonical assembled evidence substrate. Upstream retrieval results get converted into a ranked, normalized, chunked bundle.

Citation derivation
Next durable artifact:

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2

Its metadata shows:

derivation_contract_id: aps_evidence_citation_derivation_v1

bundle_ref: ...aps_evidence_bundle_v2.json

snapshot_contract_id: aps_evidence_snapshot_v1

So the citation pack is derived from the evidence bundle, with snapshots preserving exact evidence state.

Evidence report assembly
Next durable artifact:

aps_evidence_report_v1

Its metadata shows:

assembly_contract_id: aps_evidence_report_assembly_v1

This is where the citation-derived evidence becomes a report object rather than just a ranked evidence graph.

Report export / rendering
Next durable artifact:

aps_evidence_report_export_v1

Its metadata shows:

render_contract_id: aps_evidence_report_export_render_v1

template_contract_id: aps_evidence_report_export_markdown_template_v1

sectioning_contract_id: aps_evidence_report_sectioning_v1

evidence_report_ref: ...aps_evidence_report_v1.json

citation_pack_ref: ...aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2.json

So this stage is the presentation/export layer. It takes the assembled report plus citations and produces a rendered export, apparently markdown.

Context packet synthesis
Next durable artifact:

aps_context_packet_v1

Its metadata shows:

fact_grammar_contract_id: aps_context_packet_fact_grammar_v1

projection_contract_id: aps_context_packet_projection_v1

source_ref: ...aps_evidence_report_export_v1.json

source_evidence_report_ref: ...aps_evidence_report_v1.json

This is important: the pipeline does not stop at report export. It projects the exported report into a structured context packet.

Context dossier composition
Farthest downstream artifact I can prove:

aps_context_dossier_v1

Its metadata shows:

composition_contract_id: aps_context_dossier_manifest_v1

fact_grammar_contract_id: aps_context_packet_fact_grammar_v1

projection_contract_id: aps_context_packet_projection_v1

That strongly suggests:

context packets are composed into a dossier

dossier is the final synthesis product in this lineage

What is probably the furthest downstream?
From the lineage I can actually see, the furthest downstream object is:

Internal terminal artifact: aps_context_dossier_v1

If you mean “furthest user-facing deliverable,” then it is:

User-facing export artifact: aps_evidence_report_export_v1

Those are not the same thing. The export is a rendered report. The dossier is a later synthesis layer.
What the repo architecture implies
Industry expert
This is a contract-driven artifact DAG, not a thin request/response API wrapper. The APS call is just the upstream source. The real system is a multi-stage evidence-processing pipeline with durable intermediate artifacts.
Data-driven researcher
The observable contract sequence is stable enough to reconstruct:

request normalization

normalization/chunking/content-unit extraction

ranking

bundle

citation derivation

report assembly

export/render

context packet projection

dossier composition

Also, the repo shows mixed versions:

upstream evidence artifacts often appear as v2

downstream report/context artifacts remain mostly v1

That usually means upstream ingestion evolved while downstream synthesis contracts stayed stable.
Contrarian innovator
The interesting part is not “calling NRC APS.” That is trivial. The real leverage is the artifact discipline:

evidence is snapshotted

citations are derived as first-class objects

report export is separated from report assembly

dossiering is downstream of reporting

That makes the system auditable and recomposable.
Skeptic
I cannot prove the exact runtime orchestration code path from handler to handler, because the connector failed on direct reads after search/list succeeded. So I can defend the artifact lineage, but not every source file implementation detail.
Also, I saw sibling artifacts like:

aps_deterministic_challenge_artifact_v1

aps_deterministic_insight_artifact_v1

I cannot prove whether those are downstream of the dossier or parallel analytical branches. So I am not calling them the terminal node.
Realist
If you need the operational summary, use this mental model:
NRC APS data enters as a normalized evidence request, gets turned into a ranked evidence bundle, converted into citations, assembled into a report, rendered for export, projected into context packets, and finally composed into a context dossier.
That is the practical answer.
One-line lineage
ADAMS APS source → normalized APS evidence request → aps_evidence_bundle_v2 → aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2 → aps_evidence_report_v1 → aps_evidence_report_export_v1 → aps_context_packet_v1 → aps_context_dossier_v1
If you want, I can next turn this into a strict DAG with node names, contract IDs, and inferred inputs/outputs per stage.
what are we working on in the processing portion

I’m taking “processing portion” to mean the middle of the APS pipeline: after raw retrieval/ingestion, before report export and dossier assembly. I’m checking which contracts and artifacts actually sit in that segment so I can answer concretely instead of hand-waving.
I’m narrowing this to the repo’s “processing” stage names rather than guessing from the high-level pipeline labels. Then I’ll map those stage names to the actual artifacts we’ve already seen.

I found the concrete processing code. This is narrower than the whole pipeline: it lives mainly in nrc_aps_document_processing.py, then feeds nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py and nrc_aps_content_index.py. I’m pulling the exact responsibilities from those files now.

Interpretation: by processing portion you mean the APS stage after raw file retrieval, but before evidence ranking/report export/context dossier assembly.
What we are working on there is not the whole pipeline. It is the document-processing core plus the ingestion/index handoff.
Processing portion = current scope
1. Media detection and routing
Main entrypoint is:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py:82-109

It detects media type and routes the document into one of these processors:

plain text

PDF

image

ZIP bundle

So the processing layer is first about type detection + dispatch, not reporting.
2. Extraction from supported document types
Same file defines the extraction contracts and extractor families:

APS_DOCUMENT_EXTRACTION_CONTRACT_ID = "aps_document_extraction_v1"

APS_TEXT_NORMALIZATION_CONTRACT_ID = "aps_text_normalization_v2"

extractor families for:

PDF text

PDF OCR

plain text

image OCR

ZIP bundle

That means the work here is:

get text out of source documents

support OCR when needed

support image inputs

support ZIP-contained documents

3. Normalization
Still in nrc_aps_document_processing.py, the plain-text path shows the basic shape clearly:

decode

normalize text

compute quality

emit normalized text + hash + ordered units + page summaries

Relevant lines:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py:119-162

So the processing layer is responsible for turning raw extracted content into a normalized, auditable internal representation.
4. Quality scoring and degradation tagging
The processing output includes:

quality_status

quality_metrics

degradation_codes

That is a strong signal that the processing portion is also where we decide whether a document is:

strong

limited

weak

unusable

Those statuses are declared near:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py:40-43

So this stage is not just extraction. It is also usability assessment.
5. Ordered units / page summaries
The processor emits structured units like:

ordered_units

page_summaries

normalized_text

normalized_text_sha256

That means the processing layer is building the substrate later stages consume. In practice, this is where unstructured files become something indexable and rankable.
6. OCR and table-processing support
nrc_aps_document_processing.py imports:

nrc_aps_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_table_parser

So part of the processing work is clearly aimed at:

OCR recovery for weak PDFs/images

better parsing of tables, likely for NRC documents with structured evidence layouts

That is probably one of the most important active subareas in processing.
7. Ingestion handoff
The processing output then gets wrapped by ingestion utilities in:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py:145-183

That file shows:

processing config generation

media detection

extract_and_normalize(...)

handoff into artifact form

It also writes:

content-addressed blobs

processing diagnostics

Relevant lines:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py:193-208 for blob storage

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py:215-230 for diagnostics path/write

So the processing portion includes not only text extraction, but also persisting the processed result and diagnostics.
8. Index gating
From grep hits in backend/app/services/nrc_aps_content_index.py, the processing result is used to build diagnostics and gate weak/unusable documents.
So downstream indexing depends on processing quality, which means this stage is the quality gate before evidence assembly.

What it is not
Based on the artifact chain we already traced, the following are downstream of processing, not part of the processing portion itself:

aps_evidence_bundle_v2

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2

aps_evidence_report_v1

aps_evidence_report_export_v1

aps_context_packet_v1

aps_context_dossier_v1

Those are evidence synthesis and reporting layers.

The clean answer
In the processing portion, we are working on:

document type detection and routing

text extraction from PDF/text/image/ZIP

OCR fallback and advanced OCR

table parsing

text normalization

quality scoring / degradation tagging

structured output generation (ordered_units, page_summaries, normalized text hashes)

artifact ingestion handoff

processing diagnostics and index gating

Framed five ways
Industry expert
This is the document-understanding layer. Its job is to convert messy APS source files into stable, machine-usable evidence primitives.
Data-driven researcher
This is the stage where signal quality is measured. The important outputs are normalized text, page/segment units, and quality labels.
Contrarian innovator
The highest-leverage work here is not basic extraction. It is robust OCR + table recovery + degradation-aware processing, because that determines recall on ugly regulatory PDFs.
Skeptic
I would not overclaim that this layer performs final evidence reasoning. It mostly prepares content for later ranking and reporting.
Realist
If you want a one-line summary:
processing = detect → extract → OCR/parse → normalize → score quality → emit structured units → store diagnostics
If you want, I can now turn this into a strict boundary diagram: retrieval vs processing vs evidence synthesis vs reporting.
explain the logic behind separating regular OCR/python libraries vs detecting heavy.detailed image content

I’m treating this as a design question about the APS processing code, not generic OCR advice. I’m locating where the repo distinguishes ordinary OCR/text extraction from “heavy/detailed image content,” then I’ll explain the rationale and tradeoffs instead of paraphrasing function names.
I’m searching for the exact branch where the code decides an image is too detail-heavy for ordinary OCR/text extraction, then I’ll explain the architectural reason for the split.

The first content search missed because the wording in code is probably more specific than “heavy/detailed.” I’m switching to the OCR/media-detection modules directly and following the decision boundary there.

Assumption: you are asking about the APS document-processing design, specifically why the codebase keeps ordinary extraction/OCR paths separate from a branch that detects documents/pages that are really image-heavy / layout-heavy / detail-heavy, instead of throwing everything through the same OCR stack.
I cannot prove the exact heuristic function name for “heavy/detailed image content” because the connector became unstable during direct reads. But the architecture is clear enough from the files I did read:

nrc_aps_document_processing.py imports separate modules for media detection, basic OCR, advanced OCR, and advanced table parsing.

It routes by media type first, then computes quality status and degradation codes.

It has explicit thresholds for “searchable enough” text and writes processing diagnostics.

That tells you the separation is deliberate, not accidental.
Core logic
The split exists because these are different failure modes.
Regular OCR / normal Python extraction
This path is for documents that are mostly:

text-first

linearly readable

structurally simple enough that PDF text extraction or standard OCR gives stable output

This path is favored because it is:

faster

cheaper

deterministic

easier to debug

easier to normalize into ordered text units

You can see that bias in process_document(...), which first does media detection and then routes to plain text / PDF / image / ZIP processors rather than using one universal heavyweight method for everything.
Heavy/detailed image content detection
This is a different problem class:

dense scanned pages

diagrams

embedded raster pages

image-dominant pages

complex tables

visually rich content where “extract text” is not the same as “understand the page”

For those, ordinary OCR/PDF extraction often produces:

fragmented text

wrong reading order

hallucinated tokens

poor table reconstruction

misleadingly “non-empty” text that is useless for search/ranking

So the system separates detection from extraction to avoid treating garbage text as valid evidence.
The repo evidence for this design
From backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py:

media detection happens before processing dispatch

unsupported content types are rejected

there are separate extractor IDs for:

PDF text extraction

PDF OCR extraction

plain text extraction

image OCR extraction

ZIP bundle extraction

there are explicit quality classes:

strong

limited

weak

unusable

That combination only makes sense if the system assumes:

not all content should go through the same extractor

extraction quality must be judged after routing

some visually complex inputs need a different handling path

The imports strengthen that reading:

nrc_aps_media_detection

nrc_aps_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_table_parser

A codebase does not split those modules unless it wants different operating modes.
Why not just OCR everything with the “best” library?
Because that is usually the wrong optimization.
Industry expert
A one-size OCR pipeline destroys throughput and auditability. Most APS documents are probably not pathological images. Running advanced vision/table logic on every file would waste time and make the common case slower.
Data-driven researcher
The model is: use the cheapest extractor that preserves evidence quality. Only escalate when signals say the extracted text is not sufficiently searchable or trustworthy. The config exposes exactly those kinds of thresholds:

content_min_searchable_chars

content_min_searchable_tokens

That is a gating design.
Contrarian innovator
The important distinction is not “OCR vs no OCR.” It is:

text recovery
vs

layout recovery

Ordinary OCR can recover characters. It often cannot recover tables, reading order, or semantically dense page structure. That is why advanced OCR and advanced table parsing exist as separate capabilities.
Skeptic
If you push all pages through advanced image handling, you may get more text, but not necessarily more truth. Complex OCR pipelines can overfit visual noise and return confident junk. Separating detection lets the system say: this page is weak/unusable, instead of laundering bad output into the evidence layer.
Realist
This is a cost-control and damage-control mechanism:

keep normal docs fast

route bad image docs differently

mark weak outputs explicitly

preserve downstream evidence quality

What “heavy/detailed image content” likely means operationally
I am inferring this part from the architecture, not quoting a single readable branch.
It likely means one or more of:

extracted text is too sparse to be trustworthy

the page is image-dominant rather than text-dominant

PDF text extraction fails or yields low-utility output

layout complexity suggests tables/visual structure matter

OCR is required but standard OCR quality is weak

the page should be treated as degraded rather than “successfully extracted”

That inference matches the repo’s explicit use of:

media-type detection

quality metrics

degradation codes

specialized OCR/table modules

processing diagnostics artifacts

Why the separation matters downstream
This is the real reason.
The APS pipeline later builds:

normalized units

evidence bundles

citation packs

evidence reports

context packets/dossiers

If early processing mistakes a diagram-heavy or table-heavy page for clean text, then downstream stages will:

rank bad evidence

cite broken spans

assemble misleading reports

pollute dossier synthesis

So the separation protects the entire evidence chain, not just OCR quality.
In one sentence
They separate ordinary OCR/Python extraction from “heavy/detailed image content” detection because text recovery and page-understanding are different problems, and mixing them would make the common path slower while letting visually complex pages silently contaminate downstream evidence with low-quality text.
The repo-level mental model
The logic is basically:

detect media/type/layout risk

use the lightest reliable extractor

measure output quality

escalate or degrade when the content is image-heavy / detail-heavy

emit diagnostics so downstream stages know whether the text is trustworthy

That is the design.
If you want, I can next turn this into a concrete decision tree like: plain PDF text → standard OCR → advanced OCR/table parsing → weak/unusable reject.

I’m looking for an actual processed artifact or diagnostics record that names a source file and flags a specific page as image-heavy/poorly searchable, rather than guessing from module names.
I didn’t find the repo via direct filesystem yet, so I’m using the project connector to locate a concrete processed artifact that points to a source file and page.

Interpretation: you want a verifiable repo example of one concrete source file and one concrete page that should go down the “heavy/detailed image content” path, not a hypothetical.
I cannot give you a trustworthy file+page pair right now, because the project connector became unstable precisely at the step where I needed to read the page-level diagnostics. Search/list worked earlier; direct reads then started failing with Resource not found for previously working tool paths. So any exact page number I name now would be invented.
What I can point to, with confidence, is where that evidence is supposed to live and why.
The source code shows that page-level processing quality is emitted by the document-processing layer, not by the later evidence-report artifacts. In backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py, the processor produces page_summaries, quality_status, quality_metrics, and degradation_codes, and distinguishes extractor families for plain text, PDF text, PDF OCR, image OCR, and ZIP extraction. In backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py, those results are written out as document-processing diagnostics under the path pattern:
nrc_adams_aps/diagnostics/<run_id>_<target_id>_document_processing_v1.json
That is the artifact you need to inspect for a real answer. The later APS artifacts you and I have already seen — aps_evidence_bundle_v2, aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2, aps_evidence_report_v1, aps_evidence_report_export_v1, aps_context_packet_v1, aps_context_dossier_v1 — are downstream and too late; they usually won’t preserve the raw page-level “this page is image-heavy / weak / unusable” signal cleanly enough.
So the honest answer is:
I cannot currently point to a verified specific file and page from the repo.
The correct place to find one is a ...document_processing_v1.json diagnostics artifact, and the page you want will be one whose page_summaries entry shows something like:

weak or unusable quality_status

OCR extractor family rather than normal PDF text extraction

degradation codes indicating poor text recoverability or non-searchable/image-dominant content

From an industry perspective, that diagnostics artifact is the right source of truth. From a data perspective, the page-level evidence is exactly there. From a skeptic’s perspective, naming a file/page without opening that artifact would be fake. From a realist’s perspective, the blocker is tool instability, not lack of a designed answer path.
Once the connector reads are working again, the next move is trivial: open the newest nrc_adams_aps/diagnostics/*document_processing_v1.json, find the first page with quality_status in {weak, unusable}, and that gives the exact file and page you asked for.
New WinRAR ZIP archive.zipZip ArchiveI attached the pdfs

I have the PDFs now. I’m unpacking the archive, checking which files are actually PDFs, and then I’ll inspect them for pages that are image-heavy or weakly searchable rather than guessing from the pipeline code.

Yes. The cleanest example from your attached set is:
File: E260226t162226_NL-25-0455.pdf
Page: 26
Rendered page: page 26 PNG
Why this page qualifies:

visually, page 26 is dominated by an engineering plot/figure, not narrative text

the text layer on that page is extremely weak: 30 extracted characters, 4 extracted words

the page contains 1 embedded image block covering about 45% of the page area

the extracted text is basically just the caption header:
Revised UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1:

So regular PDF text extraction or basic OCR sees almost none of the meaningful content. The actual payload is in the graph itself: axes, scale marks, plotted red curve, and figure semantics. That is exactly the case for heavy/detailed image content detection.
Five ways to look at it:
Industry expert: this is a figure page, not a prose page. Treating it as ordinary text extraction loses the evidence.
Data-driven researcher: the gap between visual content and extracted text is large. 30 chars / 4 words on a page with a major plot is a strong routing signal.
Contrarian innovator: the key problem is not OCRing a caption. It is recognizing that the page’s value is in the chart structure.
Skeptic: this is not “hard OCR” in the usual scanned-letter sense. It is harder: the page is semantically important but text-poor.
Realist: page 26 should be escalated because the cheap path returns almost nothing useful.
A secondary example, but weaker for your exact question, is Combined reports Dukes Health System.pdf, page 1, which looks like a scanned/filled form with signatures. That is more of a standard OCR/form-recovery case than a true heavy-detail visual-analysis case.
If you want the next step, I can mark 5-10 more pages from your archive that fall into:

plain OCR cases, versus

heavy/detailed image-detection cases.
What content/info/data can be extracted from pages 25 and 26, if anything at all, without vision?

I’m checking the PDF directly and separating text-layer extraction from anything that would require visual interpretation. Then I’ll tell you exactly what pages 25 and 26 yield in the non-vision path, including how thin that output is.

Interpretation: I’m assuming you mean pages 25 and 26 of E260226t162226_NL-25-0455.pdf, since that was the file we were discussing.
If “without vision” means no image understanding and no OCR on raster content - just PDF text-layer/object extraction - then the answer is: almost nothing semantic beyond the page headings and object metadata.
What can be extracted
Page 25
Direct text-layer extraction yields only:

[code: unknown]

Object-level data that can still be extracted without vision:

page size: 612 x 792 pt

text extracted: 41 characters, 5 words

text blocks: 2

one real heading block

one whitespace artifact block

embedded images: 1

image pixel size: 570 x 768

placed at approximately:

left: 71.21

top: 120.14

right: 508.51

bottom: 709.36

covers about 53.2% of page area

vector drawings: 1

a line from (90.75, 614.75) to (492.75, 122.75)

stroke color extracted as roughly red

width 1.0

What that means in practice: without vision, page 25 tells you only that this page is deleting the current UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1, and that the rest of the meaningful content is an embedded graphic plus a diagonal vector line. You do not get the contents of the figure.
Page 26
Direct text-layer extraction yields only:

[code: unknown]

Object-level data extractable without vision:

page size: 612 x 792 pt

text extracted: 31 characters, 4 words

text blocks: 1

embedded images: 1

image pixel size: 838 x 829

placed at approximately:

left: 71.39

top: 86.06

right: 540.61

bottom: 550.18

covers about 44.9% of page area

vector drawings: 0

So without vision, page 26 tells you only that it contains the revised version of the same figure, and that the actual figure content lives inside one embedded image.
What cannot be extracted this way
Without vision / OCR on the image itself, you cannot reliably recover:

axis labels

plotted values

curve shapes

legend entries

annotations inside the figure

numerical thresholds

differences between the deleted and revised figures

any engineering meaning carried by the chart/plot image

That is the core point: these pages are text-poor but information-rich visually.
Clean summary
Industry expert
These are figure-replacement pages. The document text layer preserves only the control language: delete current figure / revised figure.
Data-driven researcher
The text-layer signal is negligible:

p25: 41 chars / 5 words

p26: 31 chars / 4 words

Contrarian innovator
The extractable payload is not the chart content, only the fact that a chart object exists and where it sits.
Skeptic
A non-vision pipeline would falsely conclude these pages are nearly empty.
Realist
Without vision, you get:

the figure titles

page/object metadata

the existence and placement of the embedded figures

the red deletion line on p25

You do not get the actual figure content.

Assumption: you want a test prompt, not a production prompt. So the right design is one that forces the vision model to separate directly visible content from inference, extract as much as possible from the screenshots alone, and be explicit about uncertainty.
Use this:

[code: unknown]

If you want a harder benchmark version, use this instead:

[code: unknown]

Best practice for the test:

give the model full-page screenshots

also give zoomed crops of the figure area, axis labels, legends, and any dense annotations

ask it to process page 25 and page 26 together so it can compare them directly

The first prompt is better for practical extraction. The second is better if you want to stress-test hallucination control.

code_blocks.code:
You are analyzing screenshots of pages 25 and 26 from a technical PDF. Treat the screenshots as the only source of truth. Do not use outside knowledge. Do not assume anything that is not visually supported.

Your task is to extract everything that is actually recoverable from the screenshots, especially from the figures/charts/graphics, and to clearly separate:
1. directly visible text/numbers/labels
2. visually observable structure and geometry
3. reasonable low-confidence interpretation
4. anything illegible or uncertain

For EACH page, produce the following sections:

A. Page identification
- Page number
- Short page title/caption exactly as visible
- One-sentence description of what kind of page this is (for example: figure replacement page, plot, diagram, form, scanned page, etc.)

B. Raw visible text extraction
- Transcribe all clearly visible text exactly as it appears
- Include headings, figure titles, axis labels, legend labels, annotations, footnotes, stamps, handwritten marks, and small labels if readable
- Preserve punctuation, capitalization, units, and line breaks where possible
- Mark unreadable fragments as [illegible]

C. Figure/chart structure
Describe the figure without interpretation first:
- chart type or figure type
- number of panels/subplots if any
- axes present or absent
- axis titles
- tick labels and numeric ranges
- legends
- plotted lines/curves/markers/bars/shaded regions
- arrows, callouts, boxes, overlays, strike-through lines, revision marks
- whether the page appears to show a deletion/current version or revised version

D. Recoverable numeric/data content
- List every visible number, range, unit, threshold, date, identifier, or parameter that can be read from the page
- If a graph is present, extract any readable axis ranges, tick values, curve labels, and explicitly stated parameter values
- If exact plotted values cannot be read, say so instead of estimating

E. Comparison signal
If both pages are available, compare page 25 vs page 26 and state:
- what is definitely different
- what is probably different
- what cannot be determined from the screenshots
Do not claim a difference unless it is visually supported.

F. Confidence and failure modes
For each important extracted item, tag confidence as:
- high
- medium
- low
Explain what limits confidence (resolution, blur, small font, cropped area, skew, compression, overlapping marks, etc.)

G. Final evidence table
Return a table with columns:
- page
- item type
- extracted content
- confidence
- source location on page (top-left, center, lower-right, etc.)
- notes

Critical rules:
- No hallucinations.
- Do not “fill in” likely engineering terminology if it is not readable.
- If the screenshot quality is too poor for exact extraction, say “not recoverable from image.”
- Quote exact visible text where possible.
- When interpreting a graph, distinguish “visible on the graph” from “inferred from the graph.”
- Be exhaustive.

Analyze these screenshots as an adversarial extraction test.

Goal: determine the maximum amount of information recoverable from the images alone, while minimizing hallucination.

Rules:
- Only report content that is visually present.
- Every claim must be tagged as one of:
  [directly readable]
  [visually observable but not textual]
  [tentative interpretation]
  [not recoverable]
- Never use outside domain knowledge.
- Never normalize or “correct” text.
- Never estimate graph values unless they are explicitly readable from ticks/labels/annotations.

Tasks:
1. Transcribe all readable text exactly.
2. Enumerate all visible structural elements of the figure(s).
3. Extract all readable numbers, units, labels, and identifiers.
4. State what the figure appears to represent, but only as a low-confidence interpretation unless explicitly labeled.
5. Compare page 25 and page 26:
   - definite changes
   - possible changes
   - unknown
6. List the top 10 most important facts recoverable from the screenshots.
7. List the top 10 things that remain unrecoverable without higher resolution or a native PDF/text layer.

Output format:
- Page 25
- Page 26
- Cross-page comparison
- Recoverable facts
- Unrecoverable facts
- Confidence notes

turns.text:
Assess the following:
"""
- Page 25  
[directly readable] "Delete current UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1:"  
[directly readable] Y-axis label: "Nuclear Power (fraction of nominal)" with ticks: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.2  
[directly readable] X-axis label: "Time (sec)" with ticks: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5  
[directly readable] Figure caption: "Figure 15.5.1-1 Core Nuclear Power Transient for Inadvertent Operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System Due to a Spurious Opening of the Core Makeup Tank Discharge Valves"  
[visually observable but not textual] Red line graph: flat at 1.0 until ~3.5 sec, then drops vertically to ~0.05, stabilizes near baseline.  
[visually observable but not textual] Diagonal red line crossing chart area from bottom-left to top-right (not part of data).  

- Page 26  
[directly readable] "Revised UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1:"  
[directly readable] Same axis labels and ticks as page 25  
[directly readable] Caption: "Updated UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1 Core Nuclear Power Transient for Inadvertent Operation of the Emergency Core Cooling System Due to a Spurious Opening of the Core Makeup Tank Discharge Valves"  
[visually observable but not textual] Red line graph identical in shape to page 25; no diagonal line present.  

- Cross-page comparison  
definite changes:  
- [directly readable] Header text changed from “Delete current...” to “Revised...”  
- [directly readable] Caption prefix changed from “Figure 15.5.1-1” to “Updated UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1”  
- [visually observable but not textual] Diagonal red line present on page 25, absent on page 26  
possible changes:  
- [tentative interpretation] Line color or thickness may differ slightly due to image compression (unverifiable without original)  
unknown:  
- [not recoverable] Reason for deletion/revisions or author intent  
- [not recoverable] Whether the diagonal line was intentional annotation or artifact  

- Recoverable facts  
1. [directly readable] Figure number: 15.5.1-1  
2. [directly readable] Y-axis unit: fraction of nominal nuclear power  
3. [directly readable] X-axis unit: seconds  
4. [directly readable] Event described: spurious opening of core makeup tank discharge valves  
5. [visually observable but not textual] Graph shows nuclear power steady at 1.0, then abrupt drop at ~3.5 sec  
6. [directly readable] Page 25 header: “Delete current UFSAR Figure...”  
7. [directly readable] Page 26 header: “Revised UFSAR Figure...”  
8. [visually observable but not textual] No diagonal line in revised version  
9. [directly readable] Both captions reference same event description  
10. [directly readable] Revision status indicated by “Updated UFSAR” prefix  

- Unrecoverable facts  
1. [not recoverable] Exact numerical values of transient drop (no grid lines or labels at drop point)  
2. [not recoverable] Time resolution between tick marks on x-axis  
3. [not recoverable] Origin or purpose of diagonal red line in page 25  
4. [not recoverable] Software or tool used to generate graphs  
5. [not recoverable] Author names or revision dates  
6. [not recoverable] Engineering tolerances or error margins  
7. [not recoverable] Source document metadata (title, section, chapter)  
8. [not recoverable] Color codes or legends (none present)  
9. [not recoverable] Annotations or handwritten notes outside main figure  
10. [not recoverable] Physical print quality or calibration of display device used  

- Confidence notes  
All claims tagged per instruction. Text transcriptions exact. Visual observations limited to visible geometry and presence/absence. No normalization or estimation applied.
"""

I’m checking the screenshots against the claimed extraction, item by item. The main question is whether the model stayed within what is actually visible, or whether it smuggled in text/content that is only recoverable from the native PDF.

I’m assessing this against the screenshots themselves, not the native PDF text layer. The main criteria are: did it stay inside what is visibly readable, and did it interpret the graph correctly. The biggest issue is not minor wording. It is a scale-reading error that contaminates several downstream claims.
Verdict: mixed. Roughly 60/100.
The page headers, most caption text, the presence/absence of the diagonal red line, and the general existence of a sharp drop are fine. The serious mistake is that it reads the x-axis as linear 0–5 sec, when the screenshots show a logarithmic axis labeled 10^0, 10^1, 10^2, 10^3, 10^4, 10^5. That makes the “~3.5 sec” claim wrong by orders of magnitude.
Using the screenshots — page 25 and page 26 — here is the assessment.
What it got right

Delete current UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1: on page 25 is correct.

Revised UFSAR Figure 15.5.1-1: on page 26 is correct.

The y-axis label Nuclear Power (fraction of nominal) is correct.

The y-axis major ticks 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 1.2 are correct.

The page 25 caption content is basically correct.

The page 26 caption content is basically correct.

The diagonal red line is present on page 25 and absent on page 26.

Both figures depict the same event description.

The curve is roughly flat near 1.0, oscillates slightly, then drops sharply and asymptotes near zero. That qualitative shape is correct.

What is wrong
1. The x-axis reading is wrong
This is the main failure.
It says:

x-axis ticks: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

drop occurs at ~3.5 sec

But the screenshots show a logarithmic axis, with labels displayed as:

10^0, 10^1, 10^2, 10^3, 10^4, 10^5

So the correct statement is not “0 to 5 sec.”
It is “the x-axis is log-scaled in seconds, with major ticks at powers of ten.”
That also means the drop is not at ~3.5 seconds. It is at roughly a few × 10^3 seconds, visually between 10^3 and 10^4.
2. “Same axis labels and ticks as page 25” is only partly correct
The y-axis part is fine.
But because the x-axis was misread, this statement is not reliable as written.
3. “Red line graph identical in shape to page 25”
Mostly true, but too strong.
Safer version:

“The plotted transient appears visually the same or extremely similar between the two pages.”

Given screenshot-only evidence, “identical” is stronger than warranted.
4. “Text transcriptions exact”
Not true.
Why:

the x-axis ticks were not transcribed exactly

some visible page text was omitted, especially on page 25

Page 25 visibly includes footer text:

15.5-12

Revision 4

That means the output is not exhaustive, and “exact” is overstated.
What was missed
Page 25 footer metadata
Visible on page 25 and omitted:

15.5-12

Revision 4

So this claim in “Unrecoverable facts” is wrong or overstated:

Source document metadata (title, section, chapter) — partly recoverable

At minimum, a section/page identifier and revision indicator are visibly recoverable from page 25.
What is overstated in the “unrecoverable” section
“Time resolution between tick marks on x-axis”
As written, wrong.
From the screenshot, you can recover:

that the axis is logarithmic

that major ticks are powers of ten

that there are minor ticks between decades

What is not recoverable exactly is the precise numeric value of every minor tick if the screenshot resolution is insufficient. But saying time resolution is unrecoverable is too strong.
“Exact numerical values of transient drop”
This is acceptable if “exact” is emphasized.
Better phrasing:

exact values are not recoverable

approximate visual location is recoverable

Because the output itself already estimates the drop location, it is inconsistent to then imply the graph yields no recoverable numeric positioning at all.
What is acceptable but should be softened
“Whether the diagonal line was intentional annotation or artifact”
Reasonable as unknown.
But given:

page 25 says Delete current...

page 26 shows revised version

the diagonal red slash crosses the figure cleanly

A reasonable tentative interpretation would be:

likely a deletion/strikeout marker

Not definite, but stronger than pure ignorance.
Better corrected version of the key graph statements
Instead of this:

X-axis label: "Time (sec)" with ticks: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

flat at 1.0 until ~3.5 sec

It should say:

X-axis label: "Time (sec)" on a logarithmic scale with major ticks at 10^0, 10^1, 10^2, 10^3, 10^4, 10^5

Curve remains near 1.0 through the early decades, oscillates slightly, then drops sharply between 10^3 and 10^4 seconds

Bottom line
Industry expert
The extraction is directionally useful, but the x-axis misread makes the transient timing interpretation unreliable.
Data-driven researcher
The most important numeric/structural error is the linear-vs-log confusion.
Contrarian innovator
The model did fine on visible text and obvious geometry, but failed on the one thing that matters most in a technical graph: axis semantics.
Skeptic
“Exact” and “no normalization or estimation” are false. It clearly normalized the x-axis into linear 0–5.
Realist
Keep the headers, captions, diagonal-line comparison, and qualitative curve shape. Throw out the x-axis interpretation and anything derived from ~3.5 sec.
If you want, I can rewrite the quoted output into a corrected benchmark answer that stays faithful to the screenshots.
sould I hardcode or use ollama vision through API for this

Assumption: by “this” you mean extracting / comparing content from screenshots of pages 25 and 26, especially charts/figures, and deciding whether to build a fixed heuristic path or call an Ollama-hosted vision model over API.
Do not hardcode the extraction itself.
Use Ollama vision through an API, but wrap it in a deterministic harness.
The right split is:

Hardcode the workflow, schema, validation, and fallback rules

Do not hardcode the page interpretation

Why
The failure mode you just saw is exactly why.
A hardcoded pipeline can reliably do:

page routing

region cropping

OCR pass

regex extraction

sanity checks

result schema enforcement

cross-page diff rules

But it cannot robustly do:

chart interpretation

axis semantics

figure-vs-annotation separation

“is this a deletion slash or part of the graphic?”

visually dense technical-page understanding

That is vision-model territory.
My recommendation
Best architecture
Use a hybrid pipeline:

Render PDF pages to images

Crop:

full page

figure area

axis/caption/title regions

Run:

OCR / deterministic extraction on text-heavy regions

Ollama vision API on the full page + crops

Force the model to return structured JSON

Validate with hardcoded checks

Reject or flag outputs that fail consistency checks

So: vision for perception, code for control.
Why not hardcode only
Industry expert
Hardcoding is fine for stable form fields. It is bad for technical figures because layouts drift, scan quality varies, and chart semantics are not reducible to a few regexes.
Data-driven researcher
You want to minimize two errors:

false certainty from brittle rules

hallucinated interpretation from the model

A hybrid setup dominates both extremes.
Contrarian innovator
The wrong question is “hardcode or model?”
The right question is “which parts are deterministic and which parts are perceptual?”
Skeptic
Pure vision is also a bad idea. The model already misread a log axis as linear. So you should not trust raw prose output.
Realist
If you hardcode this, it will work on these two pages and then break on the next figure style.
What should be hardcoded
Hardcode these:

input pipeline

crop strategy

JSON schema

confidence thresholds

regex/consistency checks

comparison logic

escalation rules

Example checks:

if x-axis text contains 10^ or decade labels, reject any “0,1,2,3,4,5 sec” summary

if caption says “Delete current” and page has a diagonal slash, allow “likely deletion mark”

if model says “identical” but image hashes or contour differences exceed threshold, downgrade to “very similar”

What should use Ollama vision API
Use vision for:

chart/figure understanding

axis reading from screenshots

identifying whether a page is image-heavy

comparing two visually similar figures

locating differences that OCR misses

extracting text from low-quality embedded graphics where normal OCR is weak

What I would do in your case
For this exact task:

Do not hardcode page-25/page-26 answers

Use Ollama vision API

Require strict JSON

Add deterministic verification

Minimal output schema
Something like:

[code: json]

Then validate it in code.
Decision
Use Ollama vision through API.
But not raw. Use it inside a hardcoded extraction-and-validation pipeline.
Short version of the architecture choice

Hardcode: orchestration, schema, sanity checks, confidence gating

Ollama vision API: screenshot interpretation

Do not hardcode: actual page content or graph semantics

That is the robust choice.
If you want, I can sketch the exact hybrid API workflow and JSON contract for this page-comparison task.
see what would have to be replaced for this to be done. Go through everything upstream and downstream

I’m treating this as an architecture-impact request: if you swap in Ollama vision for screenshot/page understanding, what breaks or needs changing before and after that point in the APS pipeline. I’m tracing the current processing path and then mapping the replacement surface upstream and downstream.

I’ve confirmed the question is about replacement scope, not just choosing a model. I’m now mapping the current APS path into: what stays deterministic, what gets replaced by vision, and which downstream contracts would need version bumps rather than silent semantic drift.

I’m tracing the current APS code path first, then I’ll mark the exact boundary where “Ollama vision” would enter and list every upstream and downstream component that would need to change, version, or stay untouched.

Assumption: you mean using Ollama vision via API for page/screenshot understanding inside the NRC APS pipeline, not just a one-off local test script.
The important distinction is this:

If you only want a standalone screenshot inspector, very little has to change.

If you want the APS pipeline itself to rely on vision for figure/chart/image-heavy pages, the replacement surface is much larger.

The clean answer is: do not replace the whole pipeline. Replace the page-understanding layer, then version any downstream contract whose semantics change.
Recommendation first
Best path, ranked:

Hybrid replacement inside processing — recommended
Keep APS retrieval, blob storage, and most downstream logic. Add an Ollama-vision branch for flagged pages/crops.

Full multimodal replacement end-to-end — only if you want region-level visual evidence preserved downstream

Hard swap of OCR to vision everywhere — bad idea

What exists now
From the code path already inspected, the current flow is roughly:

APS source retrieval

media detection

process_document(...)

extractor choice: plain text / PDF text / PDF OCR / image OCR / ZIP

normalization

quality scoring + degradation codes

ordered units + page summaries

artifact ingestion + processing diagnostics

content indexing / content units / chunking

evidence bundle

citation pack

evidence report

report export

context packet

context dossier

The key current files are:

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py

backend/app/services/nrc_aps_content_index.py

backend/app/services/connectors_nrc_adams.py

And the key current contracts/artifacts are:

aps_document_extraction_v1

aps_text_normalization_v2

aps_content_units_v2

aps_chunking_v2

aps_evidence_bundle_v2

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2

aps_evidence_report_v1

aps_evidence_report_export_v1

aps_context_packet_v1

aps_context_dossier_v1

Upstream: what has to be replaced or extended before vision runs
1. Source retrieval from APS
Replace? No.
Keep.
You still want:

the original PDF/blob

content hashes

run IDs / target IDs

content-addressed storage

Reason: Ollama should operate on rendered page images/crops, not replace source acquisition.
2. Media detection
Current logic routes on media type and supported_for_processing.
Replace? No.
Extend.
What needs to be added:

page-level classification, not just file-level

region-level triggers such as:

image-heavy page

chart/figure page

table-heavy page

text-layer weak vs visual payload strong

new reason codes like:

requires_vision_chart

requires_vision_figure

requires_vision_layout_dense

ocr_text_insufficient_visual_payload_present

This belongs conceptually next to nrc_aps_media_detection.py, but it should become page-aware, not just file-aware.
3. Page rendering / crop generation
This is the first real new component.
Replace? Add new stage.
Currently missing or too weak for this purpose.
You need a deterministic render layer that produces:

full-page PNG/JPEG

standard crops:

title/header

caption area

chart body

axis/legend regions

stable image hashes per page/crop

Without this, “vision” is too expensive and too noisy.
New component
Something like:

nrc_aps_page_rendering.py

or nrc_aps_vision_preprocess.py

4. Document processing core
This is the main replacement boundary.
Current core:

nrc_aps_document_processing.py

process_document(...)

_process_pdf

_process_image

OCR/text extraction branches

Replace? Partially.
This is where Ollama enters.
Do not remove the current OCR/text path.
Change the logic to:

try native text extraction first

measure quality

if page is flagged as visually dense or text-poor, render page/crops

call Ollama vision

merge outputs into one processed-page object

New extractor families
You will need new extractor metadata, for example:

extractor_family = "vision_page_analysis"

extractor_id = "ollama_vision_<model>"

extractor_version = <model tag or deployment tag>

Contract version bump
At minimum:

aps_document_extraction_v1 → v2
or better:

aps_multimodal_document_extraction_v1

Reason: once you include model-generated visual observations, the semantics changed. Reusing v1 would be dishonest.
5. OCR / advanced OCR / advanced table parser
Current modules imported by document processing include:

nrc_aps_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_ocr

nrc_aps_advanced_table_parser

Replace? No, not fully.
Keep as deterministic fallback and cross-check.
Best use:

standard OCR for scanned text/forms

table parser for extractable tables

Ollama vision for:

chart semantics

figure differences

layout-heavy pages

“what is this page doing?” judgments

Do not throw these away until you have benchmarked them against vision.
6. Processing config
Current processing config includes:

page limits

OCR toggle

OCR DPI

token/searchable-char thresholds

timeouts

Replace? Extend.
You need new fields such as:

vision_enabled

vision_provider = ollama

vision_model

vision_timeout_seconds

vision_page_trigger_policy

vision_crop_strategy

vision_prompt_template_id

vision_max_pages

vision_max_crops_per_page

vision_required_json_schema

vision_confidence_threshold

This belongs in or adjacent to processing_config_from_run_config(...).
7. Diagnostics artifacts
Current diagnostics already exist:

aps.document_processing_diagnostics.v1

written by nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.write_processing_diagnostics(...)

Replace? Yes, version.
At minimum:

aps.document_processing_diagnostics.v1 → v2

New fields needed:

model name/tag

prompt template version

crop refs / image hashes

page render refs

vision confidence

validation failures

OCR-vs-vision disagreement markers

tokens / latency / retry count

This is non-negotiable. Otherwise you lose auditability.

Middle of the pipeline: what has to change after processing
8. Processed page/document object shape
Current outputs include things like:

ordered_units

page_summaries

normalized_text

quality_status

degradation_codes

Replace? Extend, maybe version.
Right now this object is text-centric.
Vision output creates new unit types, for example:

figure_caption

axis_label

legend_entry

chart_observation

region_summary

revision_marker

bbox_ref

If you only collapse vision output into a plain text summary, you can avoid deeper downstream changes — but you lose structure and provenance.
So there are two options:
Minimal option
Append vision findings as text-like units into ordered_units.
Proper option
Create multimodal units and version the schema.
I recommend the proper option if this is meant to survive.
9. Content index
nrc_aps_content_index.py sits right after processing and uses quality gating.
Replace? Yes, at least partially.
Why:

it currently assumes text-dominant processed outputs

it likely indexes ordered units and quality status in a text-centric way

vision outputs need:

modality field

bbox/crop provenance

per-observation confidence

source image hash/ref

So:

index schema changes

indexing logic changes

query-time interpretation changes

10. Normalization and content units
Current lineage references:

aps_text_normalization_v2

aps_content_units_v2

Replace? Yes, if you want true multimodal semantics.
At minimum:

aps_text_normalization_v2 stays for text

add something like aps_vision_observation_normalization_v1
or unify as:

aps_multimodal_normalization_v1

Then:

aps_content_units_v2 → v3

Why:

a chart observation is not the same as a text block

a caption extracted from the image is different from native PDF text

you need modality-aware units

11. Chunking
Current:

aps_chunking_v2

Replace? Probably yes.
Text chunking assumes linear reading order.
Vision-derived content is region-based and often non-linear.
You need chunking rules for:

page-level visual summary chunk

region-specific chunks

caption + axis + curve summary grouping

OCR text and vision text coalescing without duplication

So:

aps_chunking_v2 → v3

12. Ranking
Current evidence bundle references:

aps_evidence_ranking_v1

Replace? Extend or version.
Ranking now likely treats all units as textual evidence. That is too naive once vision enters.
Need new ranking signals:

modality

source fidelity

OCR-vs-vision agreement

confidence

whether claim is directly readable vs interpreted

whether evidence is page-global or crop-local

So either:

keep v1 with extra features if backward-compatible

or version to aps_evidence_ranking_v2

Downstream: what has to change after evidence is formed
13. Evidence bundle
Current:

aps_evidence_bundle_v2

Replace? Yes, if you preserve multimodal provenance.
Why:

bundle entries now need image/crop refs

visual observation type

confidence

direct-text vs interpreted distinction

So:

aps_evidence_bundle_v2 → v3

If you do not version this, you will silently corrupt semantics.
14. Citation derivation / citation pack
Current:

aps_evidence_citation_derivation_v1

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2

aps_evidence_snapshot_v1

Replace? Yes, definitely, if you want citations to be honest.
Current text citations are probably span/page oriented.
Vision evidence needs:

page number

crop or bbox

image hash

maybe rendered screenshot ref

claim type

confidence

whether the claim is “directly readable” or “visual interpretation”

So:

citation pack must become region-aware

snapshot schema must preserve image provenance

Likely:

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v2 → v3

aps_evidence_snapshot_v1 → v2

This is one of the most important downstream replacements.
15. Evidence report assembly
Current:

aps_evidence_report_v1

aps_evidence_report_assembly_v1

aps_evidence_report_sectioning_v1

Replace? Likely yes.
Why:

the report must distinguish:

native text extraction

OCR extraction

vision-derived observation

it should display confidence and provenance

it may need inline crop references or modality labels

Otherwise the report will make visual inferences look like direct textual facts.
So:

report assembly and sectioning should be versioned

16. Report export/render/template
Current:

aps_evidence_report_export_v1

aps_evidence_report_export_render_v1

aps_evidence_report_export_markdown_template_v1

Replace? Yes, if you expose the new evidence honestly.
New renderer/template needs to support:

modality labels

confidence display

page/crop references

“directly readable” vs “interpreted” badges

maybe image thumbnails or crop links

If you keep the old template, it will flatten multimodal evidence into plain prose and hide uncertainty.
17. Context packet and dossier
Current:

aps_context_packet_v1

aps_context_packet_fact_grammar_v1

aps_context_packet_projection_v1

aps_context_dossier_v1

aps_context_dossier_manifest_v1

Replace? Depends on how far you push multimodality.
If you collapse vision results into ordinary textual evidence before this point:

these can stay mostly unchanged

If you want modality/confidence to survive into synthesis:

they need version bumps too

Reason:

fact grammars today probably assume a text-only evidence source

once visual interpretation enters, you need modality-aware fact extraction

otherwise downstream summaries may overstate model-generated chart interpretation as plain fact

My view:
For a first rollout, keep context packet/dossier mostly unchanged.
Upgrade them only after the evidence bundle/citation/report layer is stable.

What should not be replaced
These should stay:

APS retrieval itself

raw blob storage

content hashes

run IDs / target IDs

deterministic OCR fallback

PDF native text extraction

existing degradation/quality concept

report/dossier stages if you first collapse vision output into audited text summaries

Minimal replacement plan vs full replacement plan
Option A — minimal viable integration
This is the one I would actually build first.
Replace/extend:

nrc_aps_document_processing.py

add nrc_aps_ollama_vision.py

add render/crop preprocessing

version diagnostics

extend content index

maybe keep aps_content_units_v2 if vision output is flattened into textual observations

keep most downstream artifacts unchanged

Pros:

faster

less invasive

easier to validate

Cons:

weaker provenance

loses structured visual semantics

Option B — proper multimodal pipeline
Replace/version:

processing contract

diagnostics contract

content units

chunking

ranking

evidence bundle

citation pack / snapshot

report assembly/export

maybe context packet/dossier later

Pros:

correct architecture

good provenance

future-proof

Cons:

larger migration surface

What I would replace, concretely
New/changed services

add backend/app/services/nrc_aps_ollama_vision.py

add backend/app/services/nrc_aps_page_rendering.py

modify backend/app/services/nrc_aps_document_processing.py

modify backend/app/services/nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py

modify backend/app/services/nrc_aps_content_index.py

modify orchestration in backend/app/services/connectors_nrc_adams.py

New/changed contracts
Minimum:

aps_document_extraction_v2

aps.document_processing_diagnostics.v2

Recommended if serious:

aps_multimodal_normalization_v1

aps_content_units_v3

aps_chunking_v3

aps_evidence_bundle_v3

aps_evidence_citation_pack_v3

aps_evidence_snapshot_v2

aps_evidence_report_v2

aps_evidence_report_export_v2

Five perspectives
Industry expert
Do not replace APS ingestion or deterministic OCR. Replace only the page-understanding branch and version downstream contracts where provenance changes.
Data-driven researcher
The first hard boundary is the processed-page schema. The second is citation semantics. Those are the two places where silent drift will hurt you.
Contrarian innovator
The real replacement is not “OCR → vision.” It is “text-only evidence model → modality-aware evidence model.”
Skeptic
If you only bolt Ollama onto process_document() and keep all downstream contracts unchanged, you will get plausible output with broken provenance.
Realist
Build the hybrid first:

render flagged pages/crops

call Ollama

validate JSON

keep OCR

version diagnostics

delay full downstream multimodal replacement until the benchmark is good

Bottom line
If the goal is just to make screenshot/page inspection work, replace only the processing layer and add a vision service.
If the goal is to make the APS evidence pipeline truly vision-aware, then the replacement surface runs from:

document_processing
through

content_index / content_units / chunking / ranking
through

evidence bundle / citation pack / report export

and only maybe later into:

context packet / dossier

That is the real boundary.
I can turn this into a concrete change list by file and contract ID, with “replace / extend / keep” columns.
double check

I’m verifying the replacement surface against the actual code paths rather than relying on the earlier reconstructed lineage. The main question is whether the first replacement boundary really is document processing, or whether the current APS stack already has a hidden place where vision-like page analysis belongs.

I double-checked the architecture claim against the code path we actually saw earlier. The core conclusion still holds, but my previous answer was too broad in one place.
Corrected bottom line
For this specific feature — using Ollama vision to inspect rendered screenshots/pages for chart/figure-heavy content — you do not need to replace “everything downstream.”
You need to replace or extend the pipeline at the document-processing boundary, then decide whether to flatten the vision result into text or preserve it as a first-class multimodal artifact.
That choice determines how much downstream work you need.

What I’m confident about after the re-check
From the code snippets we already inspected:

nrc_aps_document_processing.py is the real extraction/routing boundary.

process_document(...) dispatches by media type and produces:

document_processing_contract_id

quality_status

quality_metrics

degradation_codes

ordered_units

page_summaries

normalized_text

nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py wraps that processing and writes diagnostics.

nrc_aps_content_index.py consumes the processed output and gates/indexes it.

Everything after that is evidence assembly and reporting.

So the first real insertion point for Ollama vision is still the processing layer. That part of the earlier answer was right.

What I would revise from my previous answer
Earlier I implied that a serious integration would probably force replacement all the way through evidence bundle, citations, reports, and maybe dossier. That is only true for the full multimodal provenance version.
For the practical first version, much less needs to change.
Minimal practical integration
You can keep most downstream contracts unchanged if you do this:

render page/crops

call Ollama vision

force structured JSON

convert the result into ordinary textual ordered_units

store confidence + provenance in diagnostics/metadata

let the existing indexing/evidence pipeline consume those units as text-like evidence

In that case, the real required change surface is:

nrc_aps_document_processing.py

a new nrc_aps_ollama_vision.py

a new render/crop helper

nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py

probably some light nrc_aps_content_index.py changes

diagnostics schema/version

That is it.

What actually has to be replaced
Upstream
Keep:

APS retrieval

raw blob storage

content hashing

file/media detection

native PDF text extraction

OCR fallback

Add/modify:

page rendering/cropping

page-level “vision needed” trigger

Ollama vision adapter

processing config knobs for model/prompt/timeout

processing diagnostics fields for vision provenance

Processing boundary
This is the main change point.
Current logic:

text/plain → plain extractor

PDF → PDF extractor / OCR path

images → image OCR

ZIP → bundle extractor

New logic:

keep all of the above

add:

page-level visual-density / chart / figure trigger

vision run on selected pages/crops

merge native text + OCR + vision observations

This means:

the extractor layer changes

the processed-page schema probably changes slightly

diagnostics definitely change

Immediate downstream
Needs change:

indexing or content assembly only enough to ingest the extra units/confidence

Does not necessarily need immediate replacement:

chunking

ranking

evidence bundle

citation pack

report export

context packet/dossier

Those can stay if vision output is flattened into text-like evidence.

What only has to be replaced in the full multimodal version
Only if you want proper region-level visual provenance downstream do you need to version these:

content units

chunking

evidence bundle

citation pack

report rendering/template

That is not required to prove value.
It becomes required only when you want downstream consumers to know:

this fact came from a chart region

this was directly readable vs visually interpreted

this came from crop X on page Y

this had confidence Z

Without that, you can still ship a useful system. You just lose rigorous multimodal provenance.

So the corrected answer is
If your goal is:
“Can Ollama vision help us inspect screenshots/pages 25 and 26 and feed something useful into the APS pipeline?”
Then the required replacements are:

Add page rendering/cropping

Add Ollama vision API adapter

Modify nrc_aps_document_processing.py

Modify nrc_aps_artifact_ingestion.py diagnostics

Possibly lightly modify nrc_aps_content_index.py

Version processing diagnostics

Maybe version document extraction contract

And that is enough for a first pass.
If your goal is:
“Make the whole APS evidence pipeline natively multimodal and auditable end-to-end”
Then yes, you eventually also replace/version:

content units

chunking

evidence bundle

citation pack

report export/template

But that is phase 2, not phase 1.

My final position after double-checking
Industry expert
The first insertion point is still the document-processing layer. That did not change.
Data-driven researcher
The minimal viable replacement surface is smaller than I previously stated.
Contrarian innovator
Do not start by replacing citations/reporting. Start by proving value at processing.
Skeptic
There is no evidence from the code we saw that a hidden downstream multimodal layer already exists. So inventing one would be a mistake.
Realist
For your use case, the right answer is:
Replace/extend processing, not the whole pipeline.
Only replace downstream contracts later if you need full visual provenance.
If you want, I’ll turn this into a strict table:
component | keep | extend | replace now | replace later
